additionalTime: 10 minutes poblano steam time, 5 minutes cooling time, plus 20 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Roasted green salsa with deeply broiled tomatillos, poblano, jalapeno, serrano, garlic, onion, cilantro, lime, and a small amount of honey.
equipment: Bowl or foil for steaming poblano
ingredients: 1 serrano pepper, optional for extra heat
1/2 small white onion, cut into 2 wedges, plus 2 tablespoons finely chopped white onion for finishing
1/2 cup packed cilantro leaves and tender stems, divided
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 1/2 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 to 3 tablespoons reserved roasted tomatillo juices or water, only as needed
instructions: Position an oven rack 5 to 6 inches from the broiler, heat the broiler to high, and line a rimmed baking sheet with aluminum foil.
Cut any large tomatillos in half through the equator. Leave small tomatillos whole.
Place 1 1/2 pounds tomatillos, 1 large poblano pepper, 2 jalapenos, 1 serrano pepper if using, 2 white onion wedges, and 4 unpeeled garlic cloves on the prepared baking sheet.
Broil for 6 to 8 minutes, until the tomatillos are blistered on top and starting to collapse and the chile skins are blackened in patches.
Turn the poblano, jalapenos, serrano if using, onion, and garlic. Leave halved tomatillos cut-side down unless they are browning unevenly.

Broil for 4 to 8 minutes more, pulling the garlic early if it is browned and soft, until the tomatillos are olive-green and slumped, the onion is charred at the edges, and the chile skins are well blistered.
Transfer the poblano to a covered bowl or wrap it loosely in foil and let it steam for 10 minutes.
Let the tomatillos, jalapenos, serrano, onion, and garlic cool on the sheet pan for 5 minutes. Do not pour the pan juices into the blender yet.
Rub most of the blackened skin from the poblano, then remove the stem and seeds.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves and remove the stems from the jalapenos and serrano.
For a milder salsa, split the jalapenos and serrano and remove some or all of the seeds and membranes before blending.

Add the roasted tomatillos, peeled poblano, 1 jalapeno, roasted onion wedges, peeled garlic, 1/4 cup cilantro, 1 1/2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, and 3/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt to a blender.
Pulse on low speed until the salsa is evenly chopped but still slightly textured, 8 to 12 short pulses.
Taste for heat. Add the second roasted jalapeno and the serrano if using, a little at a time, blending briefly after each addition, until the salsa tastes as warm as you want it.
If the salsa is too thick to spoon, blend in reserved roasted tomatillo juices or water 1 tablespoon at a time. Stop while it is loose but not watery.
Pulse in the remaining 1/4 cup cilantro just until flecked through the salsa.
Transfer the salsa to a bowl, stir in 2 tablespoons finely chopped white onion, and let it rest for at least 20 minutes.
Taste again after the rest and adjust only if needed with the remaining 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or 1/2 teaspoon honey if the tomatillos are very sharp.
notes: The poblano is the upgrade. It adds roasted green chile depth without making the salsa taste heavy or overly spicy.
Broiling the tomatillos until they slump and char in spots concentrates their flavor better than stopping as soon as they soften.
Keep the roasted tomatillo juices out of the blender at first. Tomatillos vary in wateriness, and adding all the juices immediately can make the salsa thin and washed out.
Adding only 1 roasted jalapeno at first makes the heat repeatable. The second jalapeno and optional serrano are there for adjustment, not blind commitment.
Folding in a little raw onion and pulsing in the last cilantro after blending keeps the finished salsa bright.
Do not rinse the poblano after charring. Rinsing washes away roasted flavor.
Black pepper is intentionally omitted because it can muddy the clean tomatillo, chile, cilantro, and lime flavor.
If the salsa tastes too tart, use the optional honey before adding more salt. Tomatillos can vary a lot in sharpness.
If using a Vitamix 5200, low pulses around Variable 2 to 4 keep the salsa rustic, while Variable 6 or 7 makes it smoother.
For a mild salsa verde, skip the serrano and remove the jalapeno seeds before blending.
This salsa works especially well with breakfast burritos, pork, chicken, eggs, grilled vegetables, tacos, and chips.
title: Roasted Poblano Salsa Verde
